content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : File                                                    Date : Feb. 15, 1980

                                                            A-80-09-077

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Sarah Cameron

Subject: Filing obligations of student members of Community College
         Boards

     At the opinion request meeting of February 8, 1980 the
staff decided that student members of the boards of trustees
(or whatever) of community college districts should be required
to file statements of economic interests.

     Student members are appointed pursuant to Education Code
Section 72023.5, which provides in part that:

     A nonvoting student member shall be seated with the
members of the governing board and shall be recognized
as a full member of the board at the meetings, including
receiving all materials presented to the board members
and participating in the questioning of witnesses and the
discussion of issues.

     The nonvoting student member shall not be included
in determining the vote required to carry any measure
before the board.

     The staff thought that these provisions meant that the
student member was not "making decisions" within the meaning of
the Act, but that they were "participating in making decisions."
Under our regulations, participating in decisions includes:

     Advises or makes recommendations to the decision-
maker, either directly or without significant intervening
substantive review, by:

     (B) Preparing or presenting any report, analysis or
opinion, orally or in writing, which requires the exercise
of judgment on the part of the official or designated
employee and the purpose of which is to influence the
decision.

     It seems that the purpose of having the student members on
the governing board is just this -- to advise the board, with the
intent of influencing their decisions, by presenting the students'
opinions, or at least the opinion of the student member.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether student members of community college district boards of trustees, who are appointed as non-voting members, are required to file statements of economic interests under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Student members of community college boards should be required to file statements of economic interests because they participate in making decisions by advising the board and presenting student opinions and recommendations, which constitutes participation in decision-making under the Act's regulations.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is internal FPPC memorandum without formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION headers; question and conclusion reconstructed from narrative text. Some OCR errors present (e.g., 'Memora um', 'appo nted', 'widthout'). No formal question section found despite clear legal analysis.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether student members of community college district boards of trustees, who are appointed as non-voting members, are required to file statements of economic interests under the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: Student members of community college boards should be required to file statements of economic interests because they participate in making decisions by advising the board and presenting student opinions and recommendations, which constitutes participation in decision-making under the Act's regulations.
embedding.first_500_words: State of California Memorandum To : File Date : Feb. 15, 1980 A-80-09-077 From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Sarah Cameron Subject: Filing obligations of student members of Community College Boards At the opinion request meeting of February 8, 1980 the staff decided that student members of the boards of trustees (or whatever) of community college districts should be required to file statements of economic interests. Student members are appointed pursuant to Education Code Section 72023.5, which provides in part that: A nonvoting student member shall be seated with the members of the governing board and shall be recognized as a full member of the board at the meetings, including receiving all materials presented to the board members and participating in the questioning of witnesses and the discussion of issues. The nonvoting student member shall not be included in determining the vote required to carry any measure before the board. The staff thought that these provisions meant that the student member was not "making decisions" within the meaning of the Act, but that they were "participating in making decisions." Under our regulations, participating in decisions includes: Advises or makes recommendations to the decision- maker, either directly or without significant intervening substantive review, by: (B) Preparing or presenting any report, analysis or opinion, orally or in writing, which requires the exercise of judgment on the part of the official or designated employee and the purpose of which is to influence the decision. It seems that the purpose of having the student members on the governing board is just this -- to advise the board, with the intent of influencing their decisions, by presenting the students' opinions, or at least the opinion of the student member.
embedding.summary: An internal FPPC memorandum from February 15, 1980 concluding that student members of community college district boards of trustees must file statements of economic interests, as their advisory role and participation in board discussions constitutes participation in decision-making under the Political Reform Act.
